coredata.dc:title: Selective adsorption and photodegradation of potentially hazardous salicylic acid by a novel magnetic surface molecularly imprinted photocatalyst (SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs)

Jiao, Jiao
Gai, Qing-Yan

Wang, Yuan
coredata.dc:description:
Salicylic acid (SA) is a potentially hazardous pollutant in domestic water systems. For the first time, a photocatalyst prepared by loading surface molecularly imprinted polymers onto magnetic black TiO2/carbon quantum dots (SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs) was developed for the selective adsorption and photodegradation of SA. The physicochemical characterization showed that SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs was successfully prepared. Under optimal conditions, SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs could achieve selective adsorption (imprinting factor 2.549) and efficient photodegradation of SA (degradation rate 99.49 %). The adsorption and photodegradation of SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs synergistically exerted the remarkable effect in SA degradation, which was attributed to the fact that the photodegradation reaction occurred not only on the surface of the molecularly imprinted photocatalyst but also in the imprinted cavities. Moreover, the degradation rate of SA by SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs still reached more than 90 % after six cycles, indicating that the SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs had satisfactory stability and reusability. The active species trapping experiments showed that
O
2
∙ −
and
h
+
played a crucial role in SA degradation. Overall, the SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs developed in this work showed promising potential for the degradation of potentially hazardous SA. This study also provided new insights into the design of molecularly imprinted photocatalysts with the ability to selectively degrade other potentially hazardous pollutants in domestic water systems.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Salicylic acid (SA) is a potentially hazardous pollutant in domestic water systems. For the first time, a photocatalyst prepared by loading surface molecularly imprinted polymers onto magnetic black TiO2/carbon quantum dots (SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs) was developed for the selective adsorption and photodegradation of SA. The physicochemical characterization showed that SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs was successfully prepared. Under optimal conditions, SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs could achieve selective adsorption (imprinting factor 2.549) and efficient photodegradation of SA (degradation rate 99.49 %). The adsorption and photodegradation of SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs synergistically exerted the remarkable effect in SA degradation, which was attributed to the fact that the photodegradation reaction occurred not only on the surface of the molecularly imprinted photocatalyst but also in the imprinted cavities. Moreover, the degradation rate of SA by SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs still reached more than 90 % after six cycles, indicating that the SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs had satisfactory stability and reusability. The active species trapping experiments showed that
O
2
∙ −
and
h
+
played a crucial role in SA degradation. Overall, the SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs developed in this work showed promising potential for the degradation of potentially hazardous SA. This study also provided new insights into the design of molecularly imprinted photocatalysts with the ability to selectively degrade other potentially hazardous pollutants in domestic water systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Pharmaceutical and personal care products (PPCPs) are a specific class of emerging pollutants with potential toxicological effects in ecological systems [1,2]. Salicylic acid (SA) is one of the most widely used PPCPs in pharmaceutical intermediates, food preservatives, and cosmetic additives [1,3]. Due to its poor biodegradability, the discharge of wastewater containing SA into the environment can cause damage to aquatic ecosystems [1,4]. Notably, SA levels of 1225 ng·L−1 and 16.6 −41.2 ng·L−1 have been reported in groundwater and tap water, respectively [1,4–8]. It is evident that SA has become a typical pollutant widely distributed in domestic water systems. However, long-term exposure or consumption of domestic water containing SA can have serious effects on human health [9–11]. Therefore, it is necessary to find an efficient, safe, and convenient method to degrade SA in domestic water systems.

SA has been reported to be removed by chemical adsorption, anodic oxidation, electrochemical degradation, and photocatalytic degradation [4,5,12–15]. Chemical adsorption only adsorbs SA on the surface of the material without achieving complete degradation. Anodic oxidation and electrochemical degradation are limited in their application due to high energy consumption, narrow pH range, and high external current requirements [4,5,12–15]. Among them, photocatalytic degradation is highly appreciated due to its mild reaction conditions and low secondary pollution [15–19]. However, the short lifetime of the free radicals generated by photocatalysts limits them to degrading pollutants in the vicinity of their generation sites [20–23]. In addition, the free radicals can preferentially degrade non-target organics and coexisting inorganic ions (such as
CO
3
2 −
and Cl-) near the photocatalyst surface, while lacking selectivity to mineralize the target organic pollutants [20,24,25]. Therefore, the conventional photocatalysts suffer from the disadvantages of low degradation efficiency and poor selectivity towards the target pollutants.

To overcome the shortcomings of conventional photocatalysts, molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) have been loaded onto the surface of photocatalysts to construct surface molecularly imprinted photocatalysts for specific adsorption and efficient photodegradation of water pollutants [26]. Zhou et al. prepared a surface molecularly imprinted photocatalyst (MICST) capable of selective adsorption and photodegradation of bisphenol A [26]. Sun et al. synthesized a novel surface molecularly imprinted photocatalyst (ATMIP), which could achieve the selective adsorption and photodegradation of methylene blue [27]. Zhang et al. developed a new surface molecularly imprinted photocatalyst (MIP-3) for the selective photodegradation of ceftriaxone sodium [28]. The SMIPs in these photocatalysts could accurately adsorb the target pollutants to the vicinity of photocatalytic active sites, and significantly improve the degradation efficiency. Therefore, it is highly expected to prepare the surface molecularly imprinted photocatalyst to selectively identify and degrade SA from water samples.

For decades, TiO2 has been considered as the most popular photocatalyst [13]. However, the free radicals are generated by TiO2 only under UV irradiation, and the photoelectrons and holes tend to recombine easily, which severely limits the photocatalytic activity [13,27]. Black TiO2 (b-TiO2) offers lower bandgap and higher utilization of visible light, making it a promising alternative to TiO2 [29–31]. Defect species such as oxygen vacancies (OVs) and Ti3+ in b-TiO2 can reduce its bandgap width and extend its light absorption into the visible range, thus resulting in improved photocatalytic performance under visible light [29–31]. However, the photocatalytic efficiency of b-TiO2 is far from flawless due to the rapid recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes [30]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have excellent electronic storage and transfer capacity, and the combination of CQDs with b-TiO2 can improve the conductivity of composites and enhance the separation of electron-hole pairs [29]. Therefore, the photocatalytic performance of b-TiO2 is expected to be further enhanced by combining it with CQDs.

In photocatalytic degradation, the powdery photocatalysts are difficult to recycle, thus resulting in secondary pollution [32]. The preparation of magnetic photocatalysts is a convenient approach for their recovery [32]. Nano zero valent iron (nZVI, Fe0) is a promising nanomaterial for the remediation of organic pollutants due to its strong reduction capacity, magnetism, and biocompatibility [30,32]. The introduction of Fe0 can facilitate the separation and recovery of photocatalysts under external magnetic fields [30,32]. In addition, when combined with b-TiO2, the oxide layer (Fe2O3) on the Fe0 surface can serve as a typical semiconductor to form a heterojunction [29,30]. This not only suppresses the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes, but also contributes to extending the light absorption wavelength range of the photocatalyst [29,30,33,34].

In the present study, the magnetic photocatalyst (Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs) was initially prepared by hydrothermal reaction and magnetization. Then, MIPs were loaded onto Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs to prepare a novel magnetic surface molecularly imprinted photocatalyst (SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs), which aimed to achieve the selective adsorption and photodegradation of SA in water. The characterization, adsorption performance, photocatalytic activity, and degradation mechanism of SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs were systematically investigated. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study to develop a promising photocatalyst (SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs) composed of MIPs, Fe0, b-TiO2, and CQDs for the selective adsorption and photodegradation of SA. The results of this study are useful for the degradation of potentially hazardous SA from domestic water systems.

Salicylic acid (C7H6O3, SA, ≥ 99.0 %), ethanol (C2H6O, EtOH, ≥ 99.5 %), acetone (C3H6O, ≥ 99.5 %), acetonitrile (ACN, ≥ 99.5 %), 4-vinylpyridine (4-VP, 96.0 %), ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA, 98.0 %), 2,2’-azobi (2-methylpropionitrile) (AIBN, 99.0 %), methanol (CH4O, MeOH, ≥ 99.5 %), and acetic acid (C2H4O2, ≥ 99.5 %) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Tetrabutyl titanate (C16H36O4Ti, TBOT, ≥ 99.0 %), isopropanol (C3H8O, IPA, ≥ 99.5 %), manganese acetate tetrahydrate (MnC4H6O4·4 H2O, 99.0 %), citric acid (C6H8O7, ≥ 99.5 %), urea (CH4N2O, 99.0 %), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, ≥ 96.0 %), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 36 −38 %), p-hydroxybenzoic acid, (C7H6O3, p-HBA, 99.5 %), p-benzoquinone (C6H4O2, BQ, 97.0 %), and disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetate dihydrate (EDTA-2Na, ≥ 99.0 %) were bought from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Ferrous sulfate heptahydrate (FeSO4·7 H2O, ≥ 99.0 %) and potassium borohydride (KBH4, ≥ 98.0 %) were purchased from Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd.

The synthesis of b-TiO2/CQDs and Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs was carried out according to the previous reports [29,31]. In order to obtain the optimal degradation rate of SA, different masses of b-TiO2 (300 mg, 500 mg, 700 mg, and 900 mg) were used to synthesize b-TiO2/CQDs-1, b-TiO2/CQDs-2, b-TiO2/CQDs-3, and b-TiO2/CQDs-4, respectively. In addition, the mass ratios of FeSO4·7 H2O and b-TiO2/CQDs were investigated. Different mass ratios of FeSO4·7 H2O and b-TiO2/CQDs (3: 7, 3: 9, and 3: 11) were used to prepare Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs-1, Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs-2, and Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs-3, respectively.

First, 13.8 mg of template molecule (SA) and 43.44 μL of functional monomer (4-VP) were dissolved in 50 mL of ACN. Then a certain amount of Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs, 190.33 μL of cross-linking agent (EGDMA), and 7.5 mg of initiator (AIBN) were added to the above solution. The reaction bottle was sealed at 70°C, and stirred for 12 h to polymerize. The mixture was purged with dry nitrogen gas. The resulting polymer was washed with methanol/acetic acid (v/v = 9: 1) to remove SA until no SA was detected in the eluent. Finally, the composite was washed with MeOH and vacuum dried to obtain SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs ( Fig. 1). Different masses of Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs (150 mg, 250 mg, 350 mg, and 450 mg) were used to synthesize SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs-1, SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs-2, SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs-3, and SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs-4, respectively. As a control, the composite prepared without the addition of template molecule (SA) was SNIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were taken on a Apreo C microscope (Thermoscientific, USA). Wide-angle X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on a X́ Pert PRO powder diffractometer (Panalytical, Netherlands). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were analyzed using a Nicolet iS20 spectrometer (Thermoscientific, USA). UV–vis diffuse reflectance absorption spectroscopy was measured using a UV-3600i Plus spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a Krayos AXIS Ultra DLD photoelectron spectroscopy (Shimadzu, Japan). Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) and electron spin resonance (ESR) spectrometry was performed on a EMXplus-6/1 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). Ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) absorption spectra were recorded on a UV3600 plus spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area was measured by N2 adsorption/desorption on a Autosorb IQ-Mp analyzer (Quantachrome, USA). A UHPLC-Q-Exactive Orbitrap mass spectrometer (Thermoscientific, USA) was used to analyze the intermediates formed during the photodegradation of SA.

Adsorption experiments were carried out at room temperature. In the kinetic experiments, 25 mg of SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs and SNIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs were individually mixed with 100 mL of SA solution (10 mg·L−1). In the isothermal experiments, 25 mg of SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs and SNIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs were individually dispersed in 100 mL of SA solution with different concentrations (10 mg·L−1, 20 mg·L−1, 30 mg·L−1, 50 mg·L−1, 100 mg·L−1, 200 mg·L−1, and 300 mg·L−1). During the kinetic and isothermal experiments, the solutions were stirred in the dark, and samples were taken every 5 min. After centrifugation, the supernatants were measured using the UV-Vis spectrophotometer at a wavelength of 297 nm. The concentration of SA was calculated from the calibration curve.

The adsorption capacity of SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs or SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs for SA was calculated using the formula Eq. (1):

To investigate the selectivity of SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs and SNIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs for SA, p-HBA was chosen as a competitive substance as its structure is quite analogous to SA. In the dark, 25 mg of material was added to a single or binary solution (100 mL) of SA and/or p-HBA (10 mg·L−1). The absorbances of SA and p-HBA were measured using the UV-Vis spectrophotometer at 297 nm and 247 nm, respectively. The selective recognition of SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs for SA was evaluated by the imprinting factor (IF) and the selectivity coefficient (SC), which were calculated according to formulas Eq. (6) and Eq. (7), respectively:

c are the imprinting factors of SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs for SA and p-HBA, respectively.

The 300 W xenon lamp equipped with an ultraviolet cut-off filter (CEL-HXF300-T3, Beijing China Education Au-Light Technology co., LTD., λ = 350–780 nm) was used as the light source. The molecularly imprinted photocatalyst was added to the single or binary solution (100 mL) of SA and/or p-HBA (10 mg·L−1). After adsorption in the dark (1 h), the solution was placed under the light source and samples were taken every 30 min. After centrifugation, the supernatants were measured using the UV-Vis spectrophotometer at 297 nm and 247 nm, respectively. The degradation rate of SA was calculated using the Eq. (8):

The effect of the amounts of SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs (20 mg, 25 mg, and 30 mg) and the initial concentrations of SA (8 mg·L−1, 10 mg·L−1, and 15 mg·L−1) on the degradation of SA was investigated. In addition, the photocatalytic activities of SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs, SNIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs, and Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs for SA degradation were compared. The selective degradation performance of SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs was investigated in the single or binary solution of SA and/or p-HBA. The effect of initial pH on the photocatalytic activity of SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs was investigated. The initial pH of the SA solution was adjusted by HCl or NaOH solution.

To evaluate the stability and reusability of SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs, the photocatalyst was reused in several cycles under the optimal degradation conditions (25 mg of SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs and 10 mg·L−1 of SA). At the end of each cycle, the photocatalyst was collected using a magnet. The resulting photocatalyst was sequentially washed with methanol/acetic acid (v/v = 9: 1), ultrapure water (UPW), and EtOH, and vacuum dried at 60°C for 12 h. Finally, the recovered photocatalyst was used for the next cycle. In order to further verify the stability and reusability of SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs, SEM, XRD, and XPS characterization were carried out to investigate the morphological and chemical state alterations between the SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs after six cycles and the new one.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of active species and degradation intermediates
ce:section.ce:para:
The trapping experiments were carried out to distinguish the main active species in the photocatalytic reaction mediated by SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs. After the adsorption of SA by SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs in the dark, the quenchers including BQ, IPA, and EDTA-2Na (4 mmol·L−1, 8 mL) were added to the reaction system to trap
O
2
∙ −
, ·OH, and holes (
h
+
). In addition, the degradation intermediates were detected under optimal conditions, after 6.5 h of photodegradation, the solution was centrifuged and passed through a 0.22 μm filter membrane, and the intermediate products were detected on the UHPLC-Q-Exactive Orbitrap mass spectrometer.

As shown in Fig. S1a and S1b, the addition amount of b-TiO2 and Fe0 to prepare SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs had a great influence on the degradation of SA. When the amount of b-TiO2 was 700 mg, the obtained b-TiO2/CQDs-3 showed the optimal degradation of SA (Fig. S1a). As shown in Fig. S1b, the degradation rate of SA decreased after the introduction of Fe0, which could be attributed to the lower b-TiO2 content in Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs compared to the same mass of b-TiO2/CQDs. This was consistent with other research findings [35]. When the mass ratio of FeSO4·7 H2O to b-TiO2/CQDs-3 was 3: 9, the degradation rate of SA by Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs-2 reached the highest value (90.50 %). Therefore, Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs-2 was selected for the preparation of surface molecularly imprinted photocatalysts.

In this study, the loading of MIPs onto Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs was found to have a significant effect on the adsorption-photocatalytic performance of the prepared SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs. Accordingly, the effect of different amounts of Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs (150 mg, 250 mg, 350 mg, and 450 mg) on the adsorption-photocatalytic performance of SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs was investigated. As shown in Fig. 2, the adsorption-photocatalytic property of SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs-3 was found to be best when the addition amount of Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs was 350 mg. The insufficient addition of Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs resulted in excessive self-polymerization of MIPs, which would reduce the imprinted sites loaded onto the photocatalyst surface [36]. Meanwhile, the excessive addition of Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs would cause the limited loading of MIPs onto the photocatalyst surface [36]. Both cases resulted in the inferior adsorption-photocatalytic performance of the prepared SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs.

Fig. 3a-d shows the SEM images of Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs and SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs. Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs was spherical with uniformly sized particles on the surface (Fig. 3a and b). These particles could be attributed to b-TiO2 covering the surface of Fe0. Compared to Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs, SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs showed a certain degree of agglomeration and a decrease in surface roughness, but there were still a number of b-TiO2 particles on the surface (Fig. 3c and d). This indicated that MIPs was successfully loaded onto Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs and a certain amount of b-TiO2 photocatalytic sites were also retained. The result of energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) showed the presence of Ti, Fe, C, and O elements in SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs (Fig. 3e-k). In addition, the EDS result (Fig. S2) of b-TiO2/CQDs showed that the C content was 5.79 wt%, which was attributed to the introduction of CQDs. The increase in C content in SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs could be attributed to the presence of MIPs.

Fig. 4a shows the XRD patterns of b-TiO2, b-TiO2/CQDs, Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs, SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs, and SNIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs. All samples had the diffraction peaks of TiO2 [29]. This reflected that the crystal structure of b-TiO2 did not change with the color variation. For Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs, SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs, and SNIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs, the characteristic peaks at 2θ of 44.6° and 64.9° corresponded to (110) and (211) planes of Fe0, respectively [29,37]. Due to the amorphous structure of CQDs, no diffraction peaks of CQDs were observed in the XRD patterns of b-TiO2/CQDs, Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs, SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs, and SNIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs [31,38]. In addition, there was no obvious shift in the b-TiO2 peak, suggesting that the introduction of CQDs, Fe0, and MIPs did not affect the crystal structure of b-TiO2.

As shown in Fig. 4b, the broad peaks at 500 cm−1 were attributed to Ti-O-C and/or Ti-O-Ti stretching vibrations for all samples [29,39,40]. The interaction between the carboxyl groups of CQDs and the hydroxyl group of TiO2 (001) resulted in the formation of the Ti-O-C bond, proving the successful doping of CQDs [41]. The absorption bands around 1000 cm−1 and 630 cm−1 corresponded to the Fe-O-C bond and the stretching vibration modes of Fe-O, respectively, demonstrating the presence of iron oxides [29,39]. For SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs and SNIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs, the CO, C-N, and C-O bonds were caused by the absorption peaks of 4-VP and EGDMA, indicating that the MIPs were successfully loaded onto the surface of Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs [26,29,42–44]. In addition, no characteristic peaks of the template molecule SA were found, indicating that SA in SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs was completely removed after elution. The characteristic peak of Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs was observed in SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs, confirming that the crystal structure of Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs did not change significantly before and after MIPs loading.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–vis diffuse reflectance absorption spectroscopy analysis
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 4c, the light absorption range of b-TiO2 and SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs covered the UV and visible-light regions. Compared to b-TiO2, SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs had superior UV and visible-light absorption capacity. This was attributed to the introduction of CQDs, which promoted the conversion of Ti4+ to Ti3+ and the formation of Ti-O-C bonds, thus increasing the light absorption range and strength [15,45,46]. As shown in Fig. 4d, the bandgap energies of b-TiO2 and SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs are 2.36 and 1.89 eV, respectively. Compared to b-TiO2, SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs had a lower bandgap energy suggesting that SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs could be excited to generate more electron-hole pairs and exhibit higher photocatalytic activity under visible light [47].

As shown in Fig. S3, SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs was found to be composed of Ti, Fe, C, and O elements. As shown in Fig. 5a, the Ti 2p spectrum consisted mainly of Ti3+and Ti4+ [15,48]. As exhibited in Fig. 5b, the three peaks of the Fe 2p spectrum belonged to Fe0 and Fe2O3, indicating that the surface of Fe0 was covered by a thin layer of Fe2O3 [29,31,39]. This was consistent with the results of FT-IR spectra. As presented in Fig. 5c, the C 1s spectra of SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs could be fitted into three peaks [15,42,49]. Among them, the characteristic peak at 284.8 eV was associated with the C-C bond of CQDs [38]. The presence of Ti-O-C bonds was attributed to the substitution of oxygen atoms by carbon atoms in TiO2 lattice [42]. As shown in Fig. 5d, lattice oxygen (Ti-O lattice), OVs, and hydroxyl oxygen (O OH) were found to be present in the O 1s spectrum [15,39]. Hydroxyl oxygen could be attributed to OH adsorbed on the surface of SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs by breaking water molecules [55]. Hydroxyl oxygen could not only enhance the adsorption of SA by SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs, but also capture holes to produce ·OH, thus enhancing the photocatalytic activity [15,42]. In addition, the strong signals at 1.998 and 2.003 in EPR spectra further confirmed the presence of Ti3+and OVs in SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs (Fig. S4) [50]. The strong signal centered at g = 2.002 indicated that unpaired electrons occupied the internal Ti positions, leading to the formation of Ti3+defects in b-TiO2 [50]. Furthermore, the introduction of CQDs could cause some of the Ti4+ to be reduced to Ti3+ [49]. The generation of OVs was attributed to surface defects in the vicinity of Ti3+ [15]. The presence of Ti3+ defects could act as active sites for oxygen adsorption and for trapping electrons from the electron-hole recombination, thereby enhancing the photocatalytic activity [15,51,52]. The presence of Ti3+ and OVs could introduce new defect energy levels in the range of 0.7–1.2 eV below the conduction band (CB) of b-TiO2, resulting in a reduction in the bandgap width of b-TiO2. Specifically, the lattice defects could cause lattice distortion, thereby altering the band structure of the crystal and reducing the bandgap [53,54]. In addition, Ti3+and OVs can form impurity bandgaps lower than the CB of b-TiO2 [53,54]. Therefore, the presence of OVs and Ti3+ could reduce the bandgap width of SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs, thereby enhancing the photocatalytic activity [53,54]. Overall, the above physicochemical characterization indicated that SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs was successfully prepared. And, it was expected that the composite would have promising adsorption and photocatalytic properties.

As shown in Fig. 6a, the adsorption capacities of SA on SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs and SNIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs increased rapidly within 5 min, and the adsorption equilibrium was gradually established within 30 min. At equilibrium, the adsorption capacity of SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs (5.385 mg·g−1) for SA was about 2.557 times higher than that of SNIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs (2.106 mg·g−1), demonstrating the superiority of MIPs for the selective adsorption of SA. Due to the presence of imprinted cavities, the surface area of SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs (73.775 m2·g−1) was found to be higher than that of SNIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs (31.737 m2·g−1) (Table S1), which also contributed to the enhanced adsorption capacity of SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs towards SA. Furthermore, the kinetic results showed that the adsorption of SA on SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs was better fitted by the pseudo-second-order model ( Table 1), indicating that chemical adsorption was the main controlling process [56].

As shown in Fig. 6b, the adsorption capacities of SA on SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs and SNIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs increased with the increase of SA concentration. In addition, the adsorption capacity of SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs for SA was found to be much higher than that of SNIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs, which demonstrated again that the presence of MIPs was indeed conducive to the targeted adsorption of SA. In this regard, it was expected that SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs would have a better photodegradation ability for SA compared to SNIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs. This conclusion would be confirmed in the following experiments. In addition, the isotherm results showed that the adsorption of SA on SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs was better fitted by the Langmuir isotherm model ( Table 2), indicating that the adsorption of SA on SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs belonged to the monolayer adsorption behavior.

To test the adsorption selectivity of SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs and SNIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs towards SA in single and binary solution systems, p-HBA with similar molecular structure was selected as a competitive analogue [57]. Compared to SNIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs, SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs had significantly higher adsorption capacities for SA in both single and binary solution systems (Fig. 6c and d). In addition, both SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs and SNIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs had low adsorption capacities for p-HBA. The selective recognition ability of SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs was evaluated by IF and SC, and the results are shown in Table S2. The IF (2.549) and SC (2.559) values of SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs for SA in both single and binary solution systems were much higher than those of SNIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs. Therefore, it could be concluded that the imprinted cavities on the surface of SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs could be well-matched to the molecular structure and functional groups of SA, thus contributing to the specific recognition of SA in complex systems containing competitive compounds.

In this work, SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs was designed to achieve selective adsorption and efficient photodegradation of SA. As shown in Fig. 7a and b, the effect of SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs dose and initial SA concentration on the photodegradation of SA was investigated. The degradation rate of SA was found to be optimal (99.49 %) when the dose of SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs was 25 mg and the concentration of SA was 10 mg·L−1. For a given concentration of SA (10 mg·L−1), the low degradation rate of SA by the excess photocatalyst (30 mg of SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs) could be attributed to the increase in light scattering effect, thus resulting in a decrease in light utilization by the photocatalyst [28]. For a fixed dose of SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs (25 mg), the lower concentration of SA (8 mg·L−1) caused the poor adsorption of SA on the surface molecularly imprinted photocatalyst, thus weakening the photodegradation efficiency. However, the higher concentration of SA (15 mg·L−1) resulted in the formation of more degradation intermediates, thus inhibiting the photocatalytic activity in a feedback manner [28,47]. Consequently, 25 mg of SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs and 10 mg·L−1 of SA were selected for the following photodegradation study.

SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs was compared with SNIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs and Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs to evaluate its advantages in terms of adsorption and photodegradation of SA. As shown in Fig. 7c, the degradation rates of SA by SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs, SNIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs, and Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs were 99.49 %, 60.28 %, and 90.50 %, respectively. The best degradation performance of SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs was attributed to the presence of specific imprinted cavities, which enabled the enrichment of SA on the surface of Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs for efficient photodegradation of SA. Indeed, the MIPs covering the surface of Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs reduced the exposure of photocatalytic active sites (Fig. 3c and d). However, the excellent adsorption of SA by MIPs enhanced the contact between the photocatalytic active sites of Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs and SA, which synergistically enhanced the photodegradation of SA [36]. As for SNIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs, the photocatalytic active sites were covered by non-imprinted polymers, which could not selectively adsorb SA, thus resulting in a much lower degradation efficiency of SA than those of SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs and Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs. In addition, the absorption intensity at 297 nm decreased gradually and disappeared in the SA solution treated by SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs (Fig. 7d), which provided the visual results that the prepared surface molecularly imprinted photocatalyst possessed excellent photocatalytic degradation performance for SA. Furthermore, the advantages of the SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs developed in this work over other materials reported in the literature for SA removal are summarized in Table 3. Although the catalysts reported in the literature could degrade SA, they had no selective degradation ability and were not suitable for degrading SA in complex water bodies. Adsorbent materials reported in the literature had high adsorption capacities for SA, but they could not degrade SA. Obviously, SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs had both selective adsorption and photodegradation capabilities, and can be used for selective degradation of SA in complex water bodies.

To further demonstrate the specificity of SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs for the degradation of SA, p-HBA was selected as a competing pollutant [57]. The degradation of SA and p-HBA by SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs was compared in single and binary solution systems, and the results are shown in Fig. 8a. In the single solution system, the degradation rate of SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs for SA reached 99.49 %, which was much higher than that of p-HBA (37.18 %). This indicated that SA could be enriched in the imprinted cavities of SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs for efficient photodegradation. However, p-HBA was not recognized by the imprinted cavities, and could not be accumulated on the SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQD surface, thus resulting in a low degradation rate of p-HBA [32,60,61]. In the binary solution system, the degradation rate of SA (67.37 %) was still higher than that of p-HBA (4.58 %). The present findings showed that SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs could achieve the selective recognition and efficient photodegradation of SA in aqueous systems with the competing pollutant p-HBA. In fact, pollutants in the aquatic environment are complex [61–63]. Therefore, the proposed SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs showed promising potential for the specific degradation of SA in aqueous systems.

ce:section-title.ce:italic: Effect of pH on SA degradation by SMIP-Fe

ce:section.ce:para: The pH of the solution can affect the surface charge of the molecularly imprinted photocatalyst and the formation of hydroxyl radical, thus affecting the adsorption and photodegradation of pollutants [47]. In Fig. 8b, the degradation rate of SA by SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs was investigated at different pH values ranging from 1.0 to 7.0. As the pH increased from 1.0 to 4.5, the degradation rate of SA increased from 76.12 % to 99.49 %. However, the degradation rate of SA decreased from 99.49 % to 33.93 % when the pH was further increased to 7.0. It was noteworthy that the adsorption trend of SA was consistent with the degradation trend. As the isoelectric point of TiO2 was around 5.0, the surface of TiO2 was positively charged at pH < 5.0 [64–66]. In addition, SA was present as an anion at pH > pKa (pKa = 2.97) [67]. Under acidic conditions, the electrostatic interaction allowed SA and SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs to bind, which was beneficial for the photodegradation of SA. As the pH increased, the electrostatic interaction between SA and SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs was weakened, thus resulting in a lower degradation rate of SA [68]. Moreover, the decrease in the amount of ·OH under alkaline conditions was reported to lead to the weakened photocatalytic ability [9,69]. Based on the above, the optimal pH of the solution was found to be 4.5.

The regeneration cycle experiments were carried out to investigate the stability and reusability of SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs, and the results are shown in Fig. 8c. Although the photocatalytic activity of SMIP-Fe0@b- TiO2/CQDs decreased slightly after 6 cycles, the degradation rate of SA still reached more than 90 %. The decrease in photocatalytic performance could be attributed to the occupancy of molecularly imprinted cavities by degradation intermediates, which reduced the adsorption capacity and photocatalytic selectivity of SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs [70]. In addition, SEM, XRD, and XPS characterization were carried out to investigate the morphological and chemical state alterations between the SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs after six cycles and the new one. As shown in Fig. S5 and Fig. 8d, there was almost no change in the morphological structure between the recovered catalyst and the new one. As shown in Fig. 8e-h and Fig. 5, there were indeed changes in the XPS patterns of Fe 2p and O 1s between the recovered catalyst and the new one. These alterations in chemical state were attributed to the inevitable oxidation of Fe0 in the catalyst after multiple cycles of use [29,30]. It is possible that this could be the reason for the decrease in the degradation rate of SA catalyzed by SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs after six cycles. Moreover, SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs could be separated by an external magnetic field and easily recovered from the solution (Fig. 8d). Therefore, SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs was promising for practical applications due to its superior stability and reusability.

Active species quenching experiments were carried out to identify the primary active species in the photocatalytic degradation of SA. Three typical scavengers, BQ, IPA, and EDTA-2Na were used as quenchers for
O
2
∙ −
, ·OH, and
h
+
, respectively [42,71,72]. The degradation rate of SA was significantly reduced in the presence of BQ or EDTA-2Na, whereas IPA had little effect on the degradation rate ( Fig. 9a). Therefore,
O
2
∙ −
and
h
+
were the main active species for the photocatalytic degradation of SA. In addition, ESR characterization was carried out to analyze the types of active species in the photocatalytic degradation of SA by SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs. The results showed that ·OH,
O
2
∙ −
, and
h
+
were indeed generated during the photodegradation of SA by SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs (Fig. 9b-d).

The possible photodegradation mechanism of SA by SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs is displayed in Fig. 10. First, SA molecules were specifically adsorbed onto the imprinted cavities of SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs. Under visible light, SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs would produce
h
+
and electrons (
e
−
) by valence band (VB) electron hopping Eq. (9) [42]. Due to the good electroconductivity, CQDs could trap the electrons emitted from the VB of b-TiO2, which could reduce the recombination of
e
−
and
h
+
, and improve the photocatalytic performance [29,32,42,73]. The electron acceptors such as O2 adsorbed on the surface of SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs or dissolved in water could be reduced by
e
−
to
O
2
∙ −
Eq. (10). The
h
+
migrating to the surface of b-TiO2could oxidize OH− or H2O to ·OH Eq. (11) and Eq. (12) [42,47,74]. The
h

, ·OH, and
O

To further clarify the possible degradation pathways of SA by SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs, the intermediates generated in the reaction systems were analyzed by high-resolution mass spectrometry (HR-MS) ( Fig. 11 and Fig. S6). First, SA reacted with ·OH to form a hydroperoxide intermediate, then released CO2 and H2O to form catechol and hydroquinone, and further oxidized to form 1,2-benzoquinone [3]. The 1,2-benzoquinone then reacted with ·OH to open its hexavalent ring to form muconic acid [3,75]. After ring cleavage, muconic acid could be further cleaved to form carbonyl compounds such as maleic acid and fumaric acid [8,76]. Finally, some intermediates could be further degraded gradually to CO2 and H2O [8,76]. By extending the photodegradation time, no SA and intermediates were found in the reaction systems, and it was speculated that SA and intermediates were completely mineralized to CO2 and H2O.

In conclusion, a novel magnetic surface molecularly imprinted photocatalyst SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs was successfully prepared. The OVs and Ti3+ in b-TiO2 could extend the visible-light response range, resulting in improved photocatalytic properties. The introduction of CQDs further extended the visible-light response range, hindered the electron-hole recombination, and improved the photocatalytic performance. The introduction of Fe0 made the photocatalyst easy to recycle and reuse. The loading of MIPs enabled the photocatalyst to selectively adsorb SA. The adsorption of SA by SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs was mainly chemical adsorption, and belonged to the monolayer adsorption behavior. SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs was able to specifically recognize and photodegrade SA in aqueous systems containing the competitive compound p-HBA. Under optimal conditions, the degradation rate of SA by SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs could reach 99.49 % under visible light. Compared with the reported materials for removing SA, SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs has the ability to selectively adsorb and photodegrade SA simultaneously. The degradation rate of SA by SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs could still reach 90.30 % after 6 cycles. In addition,
O
2
∙ −
and
h
+
were found to be the main active species in the photodegradation of SA by SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs. And, the photodegradation mechanism and pathways of SA by SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs were preliminarily elucidated. Overall, this study offered a promising photocatalyst SMIP-Fe0@b-TiO2/CQDs for the degradation of potentially hazardous SA. Furthermore, the present study provided references for the design of surface molecularly imprinted photocatalysts for the specific degradation of other pollutants in domestic water systems.
